ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic steamed bun class; no specific char siew pau entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class FOODON_03302050.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed filled bun — wheat dough filled with seasoned pork
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated char siew pau code. A0FKP (filled steamed bun) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for char siew pau or steamed BBQ pork bun as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard char siew pau weighs approximately 80–100 g; a typical serving is one piece (approximately 90 g).

nutrient_source.title: Char Siew Pau — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists char siew pau (steamed BBQ pork bun) as a named entry with per-100 g values. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken directly from this source. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for steamed pork bun (NDB 18350) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for char siew pau exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy to steamed white-flour buns (mantou/baozi) which are listed in the University of Sydney GI Database with GI values of 65–68 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The sweet filling and refined wheat dough composition of char siew pau are consistent with this range. GL per serving = (65 × 35.95 × 0.90) / 100 = 21.1 g per 90 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 366 mg/100 g combined with high sugar (14.34 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g), which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit intake to one piece per sitting, avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods, and note that a single 90 g pau delivers approximately 329 mg sodium — about 14% of the 2300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (35.95 g/100 g), high sugar (14.34 g/100 g), and very low dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g) producing a high glycaemic load of approximately 21 g per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit char siew pau to half a piece per occasion, pair with a protein-rich food or non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response, and avoid consuming multiple pieces in one sitting given the estimated GI of 65 and GL of 21 per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 2.8 g/100 g, trans fat approximately 0.1 g/100 g from lard or shortening in the dough, and sodium 366 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, char siew pau should be consumed occasionally and in single-piece portions; the combination of saturated fat, trace trans fat, and moderate sodium warrants caution, and lard-free or baked versions are preferable where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char siew pau (叉烧包, Cha Shao Bao) is a Cantonese-origin steamed bun that is a staple of dim sum culture and a widely consumed breakfast and snack item in Singapore, Hong Kong, and across the Chinese diaspora. The bun consists of a soft, slightly sweet, fluffy white wheat-flour dough — leavened with yeast and baking powder — that is steamed until pillowy and tender. The filling is char siew (叉烧), a Cantonese-style barbecued pork that is marinated in hoisin sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce, sugar, and sesame oil, then roasted until caramelised and slightly sticky. The result is a mildly sweet, savoury, and aromatic filling encased in a cloud-like white bun. Per 100 g, char siew pau provides 265 kcal, 10.77 g protein, 8.68 g fat, 35.95 g carbohydrate, 14.34 g sugar, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 366 mg sodium. The high sugar content (14.34 g/100 g) and low fibre (1.2 g/100 g) make this a high-glycaemic-load food (estimated GL ~21 per 90 g piece), raising concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, char siew pau is commonly sold at hawker centres, dim sum restaurants, and Chinese bakeries, and is typically eaten as a breakfast item or morning tea snack. It is not halal-certified due to the pork filling. The dish is culturally significant as part of yum cha (飲茶) dim sum tradition and is one of the most recognisable Chinese baked and steamed goods in Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a char siew pau?
Can diabetics eat char siew pau?
How much sodium is in a BBQ pork bun?
What is the GI of char siew pau?
What is char siu bao made of?
Char siew pau nutrition per piece

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which lists char siew pau as a named entry with per-100 g values at 0.95 confidence. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central steamed pork bun analogue and documented. GI estimated by analogy to steamed white-flour buns from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008).
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.5 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for steamed pork bun (NDB 18350 / SR Legacy) as HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid breakdown for this item. These values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis if available. 2. Potassium (110 mg/100 g), calcium (28 mg/100 g), iron (1.4 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (22 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central steamed pork bun analogue. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for char siew pau are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central steamed pork bun entry as of 2026. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white-flour buns (mantou/baozi) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for char siew pau has been published. 5. Halal status is false as the filling contains pork. 6. Trans fat of 0.1 g/100 g is a trace estimate from lard or shortening commonly used in traditional dough preparation; lard-free commercial versions may have lower or zero trans fat.
